text: something cool here is that there's actually a lot of value in looking back to ancient "parent tech". my friends with babies have had lots of success with simple things like breastsleeping (less wakings), elimination communication (less poopy diapers), skin-to-skin (regulation)

raw.full_text: something cool here is that there's actually a lot of value in looking back to ancient "parent tech". my friends with babies have had lots of success with simple things like breastsleeping (less wakings), elimination communication (less poopy diapers), skin-to-skin (regulation)

text: But a lot of things have been lost in the sauce of trying to modernize baby-rearing... you fix certain problems and end up causing others (thinking of diapers, for example). There's a fair percentage of the struggle that is probably necessary... growing up is not a smooth ride :)

text:
talked to a mom today who has been struggling with her son's toilet learning process at three and a half because he refuses to go to the bathroom and asks to wear a diaper so he's not interrupted when he's playing.
anyway, I feel like talking a bit abt toilet learning 👇

raw.full_text:
talked to a mom today who has been struggling with her son's toilet learning process at three and a half because he refuses to go to the bathroom and asks to wear a diaper so he's not interrupted when he's playing.
anyway, I feel like talking a bit abt toilet learning 👇

text: diapers are something we impose on children for convenience. this is well and good, but needs to be remembered when it comes to toilet learning... it is also NOT a natural process: we've conditioned the child to use a diaper, and now we're changing that.

raw.full_text: diapers are something we impose on children for convenience. this is well and good, but needs to be remembered when it comes to toilet learning... it is also NOT a natural process: we've conditioned the child to use a diaper, and now we're changing that.

text: also - disposable diapers, which absorb liquid under a 'stay dry' layer, make it so that children aren't able to get feedback from their bodies and understand the natural consequence of peeing carelessly (being wet and uncomfortable)

raw.full_text: also - disposable diapers, which absorb liquid under a 'stay dry' layer, make it so that children aren't able to get feedback from their bodies and understand the natural consequence of peeing carelessly (being wet and uncomfortable)

text: it then becomes pretty hard to teach a child to use the toilet after they've been using diapers that absorb everything and leave them feeling dry, and lost the instinctive understanding of when they are going to pee. this is something that needs to be re-learned.

raw.full_text: it then becomes pretty hard to teach a child to use the toilet after they've been using diapers that absorb everything and leave them feeling dry, and lost the instinctive understanding of when they are going to pee. this is something that needs to be re-learned.

text:
there is a progression here:
- the child is aware after the fact
- the child is aware during / right before the fact
- the child is aware before and can hold it until they get to a toilet

raw.full_text:
there is a progression here:
- the child is aware after the fact
- the child is aware during / right before the fact
- the child is aware before and can hold it until they get to a toilet

text: every child is an individual, but as a general rule, there is a sensitive period (vaguely 1,5-2 yo) and afterwards it becomes more complicated the longer you wait. I've seen many 2, 3, 4 yo's with emotional attachments to diapers or who firmly reject the disruption of their time

raw.full_text: every child is an individual, but as a general rule, there is a sensitive period (vaguely 1,5-2 yo) and afterwards it becomes more complicated the longer you wait. I've seen many 2, 3, 4 yo's with emotional attachments to diapers or who firmly reject the disruption of their time

text: montessori toilet learning is a longer, lower-intensity, more-involved approach than "bootcamp style potty training", but it's also gentler on children and creates sturdier outcomes IME.

raw.full_text: montessori toilet learning is a longer, lower-intensity, more-involved approach than "bootcamp style potty training", but it's also gentler on children and creates sturdier outcomes IME.

text: we view the adult's role as: observing progression through those stages, bringing back sensory feedback (by taking away the disposable diapers completely, or using cotton diapers / training underwear with a minimum of absorbency) and teaching the child new routines with the same

raw.full_text: we view the adult's role as: observing progression through those stages, bringing back sensory feedback (by taking away the disposable diapers completely, or using cotton diapers / training underwear with a minimum of absorbency) and teaching the child new routines with the same

text:
consistency and time as was given to teaching the child their previous routines and habits.
the adult also needs to stay neutral about the situation as much as possible, and treat accidents as a natural part of learning. there is no shame/punishment and no stickers!

raw.full_text:
consistency and time as was given to teaching the child their previous routines and habits.
the adult also needs to stay neutral about the situation as much as possible, and treat accidents as a natural part of learning. there is no shame/punishment and no stickers!

text: this 3,5yo likes to be comfortable and unbothered, and I totally get that - as far as he sees it, there's no reason to ditch the diaper! this is where parents need to fully step into the responsibility of their role as guides who can see further, and set some limits to help him.

raw.full_text: this 3,5yo likes to be comfortable and unbothered, and I totally get that - as far as he sees it, there's no reason to ditch the diaper! this is where parents need to fully step into the responsibility of their role as guides who can see further, and set some limits to help him.

text: my suggestion was to set the limits through the environment: there are no more disposable diapers to choose from, that is not an option. he will feel and then start to anticipate the inevitable discomfort and interruption that *not* going to the toilet will cause him.

raw.full_text: my suggestion was to set the limits through the environment: there are no more disposable diapers to choose from, that is not an option. he will feel and then start to anticipate the inevitable discomfort and interruption that *not* going to the toilet will cause him.

text: also relevant to add that the age of toilet learning has been increasing for decades and that disposable diapers are bad for the environment and expensive. "waiting until the child is ready" needs to be paired with proactive guidance from the parents, or it is akin to neglect.

raw.full_text: also relevant to add that the age of toilet learning has been increasing for decades and that disposable diapers are bad for the environment and expensive. "waiting until the child is ready" needs to be paired with proactive guidance from the parents, or it is akin to neglect.

text:
I love love love this so I’ll say it louder for the people in the back!!! 📢

Toddler Mgmt 101:

🙂‍↕️ be generally a Yes person - they need to explore. pick your battles. ask yourself “is this really a limit, something I’m willing to hold consistently, 100x if need be?” if not let it go
🙅‍♀️when they do cross an unacceptable limit, maintain that boundary. they will freak out. be firm and calm. practice this skill over and over (the opportunities will present themselves)
💞 connect and support. you’re stopping and correcting the behavior, not the emotion. hold the emotion with them and help them move on
🙊don’t try to verbally reason or negotiate with a young toddler. they seem way more reasonable than they are, because they can speak (kinda like an LLM lol). use physical ways to regulate, humor, silliness

this is a skill like any other, and you will get good at it. persist!! I can tell you it’s worth it 💪

text:
I remember in college studying topics like these always created some intense debates, everyone stuck in the realm of ideas. 
when you actually see it in tiny humans, while they are just joyfully and wholeheartedly being themselves, it doesn't even make sense to be angry!

raw.full_text:
I remember in college studying topics like these always created some intense debates, everyone stuck in the realm of ideas. 
when you actually see it in tiny humans, while they are just joyfully and wholeheartedly being themselves, it doesn't even make sense to be angry!

text: for example - many little boys love cars. in college I would've thought "ugh, already conditioned by society to like manly things" but after I've felt their joy and excitement for vehicles it's impossible not to think "you know what, hell yeah. let's learn all their names, child"

raw.full_text: for example - many little boys love cars. in college I would've thought "ugh, already conditioned by society to like manly things" but after I've felt their joy and excitement for vehicles it's impossible not to think "you know what, hell yeah. let's learn all their names, child"

text: I also thought I'd feel sad for little girls focusing too much on nurturing and the others around them instead of building themselves up but AGAIN once you experience their joy you realize they do gravitate towards things that feel alive and build them up in mysterious ways

raw.full_text: I also thought I'd feel sad for little girls focusing too much on nurturing and the others around them instead of building themselves up but AGAIN once you experience their joy you realize they do gravitate towards things that feel alive and build them up in mysterious ways

text: that is the secret of the child, it is not for me to know, it is only for me to observe, trust and let unfold in front of my eyes. a blossoming of interwoven genetic memories and individuality that is more beautiful than anything else, and the privilege of my life to know.

good tips here for those of you interested in infant sleep after the recent discourse.
of course - there is much variation and every baby-family organism might respond differently, but these are great general principles.

text: one good thing to remember, as with all baby-related affairs, is that that their factory settings are in many ways misaligned with our current society and lifestyle. in sleep it's clear - we want them to sleep long uninterrupted hours at night, but that's not how they're built

raw.full_text: one good thing to remember, as with all baby-related affairs, is that that their factory settings are in many ways misaligned with our current society and lifestyle. in sleep it's clear - we want them to sleep long uninterrupted hours at night, but that's not how they're built

text: this is ok, we don't need to take the "naturalistic" approach of just following their rhythm so as not to disrupt them. our role as adults is to help them adapt to society. we can choose how and when we want to do this based on our values and priorities.

raw.full_text: this is ok, we don't need to take the "naturalistic" approach of just following their rhythm so as not to disrupt them. our role as adults is to help them adapt to society. we can choose how and when we want to do this based on our values and priorities.

text: some form of sleep training ends up being necessary for many babies at some point, because they just don't get with the program on their own, but it can be as gentle as building healthy habits and sleep associations in the first months and having a consistent response afterwards

raw.full_text: some form of sleep training ends up being necessary for many babies at some point, because they just don't get with the program on their own, but it can be as gentle as building healthy habits and sleep associations in the first months and having a consistent response afterwards

text: falling sleep on one's own is a skill that many babies need to practice, and it requires that the adult step back and gradually remove support - lovingly, consistently, and keeping the long game in mind. it can look like a failure in the moment, progress zigzags.

raw.full_text: falling sleep on one's own is a skill that many babies need to practice, and it requires that the adult step back and gradually remove support - lovingly, consistently, and keeping the long game in mind. it can look like a failure in the moment, progress zigzags.

text:
the "internal posture" adults need to have with adaptation/learning processes is basically always the same, and knowing what actions to take flows downstream from it.
my best attempt at articulating it would be something like "this child is trying to integrate into the world, but there is friction. I understand things they don't, so I'm going to steadily build a bridge to help them sort out the friction."

raw.full_text:
the "internal posture" adults need to have with adaptation/learning processes is basically always the same, and knowing what actions to take flows downstream from it.
my best attempt at articulating it would be something like "this child is trying to integrate into the world, but there is friction. I understand things they don't, so I'm going to steadily build a bridge to help them sort out the friction."

text: whether you sleep train, co sleep and feed on demand, or something else entirely is up to you. everyone has different priorities and things they're willing to compromise on in the short/long term. there is no absolute or scientific way to "fix" baby sleep because it exists and evolves within a family. there are strategies you can choose from :)

text:
hey, does anyone have experience getting rid of perioral/periorificial dermatitis? I've been struggling with this for 10+ yrs, on and off, and it's super annoying.
I'm going to a new dermatologist later this month but suspect they'll prescribe the same thing as usual (oral antibiotics) and it will just come back a few months later. I would like to avoid antibiotics and treat this for good if possible!

raw.full_text:
hey, does anyone have experience getting rid of perioral/periorificial dermatitis? I've been struggling with this for 10+ yrs, on and off, and it's super annoying.
I'm going to a new dermatologist later this month but suspect they'll prescribe the same thing as usual (oral antibiotics) and it will just come back a few months later. I would like to avoid antibiotics and treat this for good if possible!

text:
things that have helped slightly but not fully:
- tetra and doxycicline
- zinc creams (cicaplast, dermalibour)
- cleansers with tea tree oil
- witch hazel
- elidel (for emergencies)

things that definitely made it worse:
- metronidazole cream
- azelaic acid
- most cleansers, moisturizers, sunscreens

raw.full_text:
things that have helped slightly but not fully:
- tetra and doxycicline
- zinc creams (cicaplast, dermalibour)
- cleansers with tea tree oil
- witch hazel
- elidel (for emergencies)

things that definitely made it worse:
- metronidazole cream
- azelaic acid
- most cleansers, moisturizers, sunscreens

text:
the dichotomy between ipad kids and living in chaos is not a thing.
YES of course messes are normal, houses get lived-in, sometimes you have other priorities!
BUT you can have good organizational systems that make restoring order easier, and "hygiene" habits such as filtering what comes into the house, frequent decluttering, rotation, nightly timed clean-ups, and you can teach children to tidy up.

raw.full_text:
the dichotomy between ipad kids and living in chaos is not a thing.
YES of course messes are normal, houses get lived-in, sometimes you have other priorities!
BUT you can have good organizational systems that make restoring order easier, and "hygiene" habits such as filtering what comes into the house, frequent decluttering, rotation, nightly timed clean-ups, and you can teach children to tidy up.

text:
in fact, if you're trying to make sure your kids get the most out of play, that they can concentrate and work on things that feel purposeful to them for long periods of time, the ideal environment is not a disorganized one with too many options.
absolutely no shade to parents here, but there are things you can do!

text: "Certainly, there is something that impels a teacher to advise very young students continually, to correct them, or encourage them, to show them that she is superior in experience and culture; ultimately she must be resigned to quelling every bit of vanity, or she will obtain no results. [...] What is expected of her is the ability to distinguish the child who has chosen the right path from the one who has erred, and she must be imperturbable, ready to be there whenever she is called in order to attest her love and confidence. To be always there - that is the point."

text: "A child who cannot keep his balance dares neither to walk nor to use his arms for fear of falling; he goes forward only uncertainly. But if he can learn to keep his balance, he will run, jump, turn right and left. The same is true for psychic life. The child who has no spiritual balance and cannot collect his thoughts has no mastery over himself; can he yield to another's will without the danger of "falling"? How can he obey another's will if he cannot submit to his own will? Obedience is nothing more than a form of spiritual dexterity that presupposes internal equilibrium." - Maria Montessori, the Child in the Family

text:
took toddler (boy) to the park today. he immediately spots groups of older boys because they are running around with scooters and footballs, and toddles after them yelling BA! BA! (ball)... but the older boys don't care for him at all.
the girls however, ALL want to hold his hand, tell him where to go and what to do, pat his head. one (~4yo) cries because the toddler is too heavy for her to pick up. they have a very serious conversation, led by the oldest one, to devise a turn-taking system for who gets to sit next to the baby. they follow the baby around like a pack, and surround him smiling and cooing whenever he stops. they ask me a million questions about him, babies in general, and me.

text: it's the difference between protecting from a few real dangers (like locking a cabinet with medicine or cleaning products, gating off the fireplace) to let baby have access to the house, and creating a small area for the baby without letting them have freedom of movement. they want to move, need to move, are curious, and barriers disencourage exploratory behavior. same reason we recommend floor beds instead of cribs and swaddling for infants only in cases of babies with extreme moro reflex, not as routine practice.

raw.full_text: it's the difference between protecting from a few real dangers (like locking a cabinet with medicine or cleaning products, gating off the fireplace) to let baby have access to the house, and creating a small area for the baby without letting them have freedom of movement. they want to move, need to move, are curious, and barriers disencourage exploratory behavior. same reason we recommend floor beds instead of cribs and swaddling for infants only in cases of babies with extreme moro reflex, not as routine practice.
